onboarding.subject: the Roman Empire
onboarding.why: to gain a better understanding of modern geopolitics through the lens of historical power structures
onboarding.summary: The user wants to study the Roman Empire to draw parallels and apply lessons to contemporary geopolitical issues.

profiling.focus_areas: geopolitical strategy, border management, power structures, and parallels to modern international relations
profiling.skip_areas: deep dives into art history or daily social life unless it directly impacts political stability
profiling.learner_context: interested in history specifically as a tool for understanding modern global politics
profiling.notes: User is extremely high-energy and wants to move fast. Keep the pace quick and the tone punchy.

course_structure.title: Imperial Grand Strategy: Lessons from Rome for Modern Geopolitics

phases.title: Laying the Foundational Power Structure
phases.description: Identify the mechanics of Roman power and how the transition from Republic to Empire mirrors modern shifts in global governance.

lessons.title: From Republic to Hegemon: The Evolution of Centralized Power
lessons.description: Analyze the systemic failure of the Roman Republic and the birth of executive dominance.
lessons.instructional_seed: Explain the shift from decentralized senate power to concentrated imperial authority, focusing on how institutional gridlock necessitates a 'strongman' figure. Compare this specifically to modern executive overreach in 21st-century democracies.

lessons.title: The Bureaucracy of Control: Managing a Superpower
lessons.description: Understand how a centralized state maintains control over vast, disparate territories.
lessons.instructional_seed: Detail the administrative layers of the Empire, focusing on tax collection and legal uniformity as tools for stability. Frame this as a study in state capacity and functional governance.

phases.title: Hard Power and the Borders of Influence
phases.description: Assess the military-industrial complex of Rome and its strategy for border security and territorial integrity.

lessons.title: The Legion as a Geopolitical Tool
lessons.description: Examine the Roman military not just as fighters, but as a mechanism for engineering and economic integration.
lessons.instructional_seed: Briefly cover military structure but pivot quickly to the legion's role in building infrastructure and enforcing the Pax Romana. Use the concept of 'force projection' to bridge the historical gap.

lessons.title: Limes and Border Strategy: Managing the Perimeter
lessons.description: Analyze Rome's physical and psychological border defenses, including Hadrian’s Wall and the Rhine frontier.
lessons.instructional_seed: Discuss the 'Limes' system as a strategy for managing migration and low-intensity conflict. Focus on the cost-benefit analysis of hard borders versus 'buffer zones' in modern strategic depth thinking.

phases.title: Soft Power and Economic Statecraft
phases.description: Evaluate how Rome used trade, citizenship, and cultural assimilation to maintain dominance without constant warfare.

lessons.title: Romanitas: Cultural Integration as a Security Policy
lessons.description: Explore how granting citizenship and local autonomy neutralized threats from within.
lessons.instructional_seed: Teach the concept of 'Romanization' as an early form of soft power. Emphasize how the expansion of Roman citizenship created stakeholders in the empire, similar to modern international alignments and alliances.

lessons.title: The Imperial Economy: Bread, Circuses, and Global Supply Chains
lessons.description: Analyze the economic interdependencies that sustained the Mediterranean world.
lessons.instructional_seed: Focus on the grain trade and currency stability as the lifeblood of the empire. Explain how economic fragility and inflation directly led to political instability, mirroring modern global market dependencies.

phases.title: Imperial Overstretch and the Collapse of Order
phases.description: Synthesize the external and internal stressors that lead to the fragmentation of a global superpower.

lessons.title: The Crisis of the Third Century: Systemic Fragility
lessons.description: Study the period of hyper-inflation, civil war, and simultaneous invasions.
lessons.instructional_seed: Analyze this period as a 'polycrisis.' Focus on how multiple simultaneous stressors (plague, economic collapse, leadership vacuum) overwhelm complex systems, providing a warning for modern globalist structures.

lessons.title: The Great Fragmentation: Why Empires Fail
lessons.description: Examine the final dissolution of the Western Empire and the lessons for modern global hegemony.
lessons.instructional_seed: Synthesize the previous lessons to show why the system could no longer sustain itself. End with a punchy comparison between the Roman 'world order' and the current rules-based international order, highlighting specific points of vulnerability.
